mappings.rationale: Directly covers the structure and properties of the universe, Hubble's law, cosmic microwave background, the Big Bang, dark matter, and cosmological models central to this section.
Directly covers gravitational theory from Newton to Einstein, the gravitational constant, general relativity, and the role of gravity in cosmology.
Directly covers special and general relativity, space-time continuum, and the astronomical implications of relativity theory discussed in this section.
Covers the large-scale structure of the universe, clustering of galaxies, and the components of the universe discussed in this section.
Covers stellar formation, evolution, and their role in the development of the universe discussed in this section.
Directly relevant to the section's discussion of quasars, supermassive black holes at the centres of galaxies, and gravitational phenomena.
Covers the physical processes governing the universe, galaxy formation, stellar evolution, and the theoretical framework of cosmology.
Covers the historical development of our understanding of the cosmos, from early models to modern cosmological observations.
Relevant to planetary systems as known components of the universe discussed in this section.
Directly relevant to the section's discussion of extrasolar planetary systems as components of the universe.
Relevant as the nearest star and central component of our solar system within the larger cosmic structure discussed in this section.
Covers fundamental physics underlying cosmological theory including gravity, relativity, and the nature of space-time.
Covers the development of gravitational theory, relativity, and the physical principles underlying cosmological models.
Relevant to Newton's law of gravity and its foundational role in gravitational theory and celestial mechanics discussed in this section.
Covers foundational concepts of space-time, relativity, and the philosophical implications of cosmological models.
Relevant to the nature of space-time, the vacuum, and the missing mass problem discussed in this section.
Relevant to primordial nucleosynthesis and the formation of elements in the early universe discussed in this section.
Relevant to the early universe, fundamental forces, and the particle physics processes in the first moments after the Big Bang.
Relevant to the age of the universe, the space-time continuum, and the time scale of significant events in cosmic history.
Relevant to questions about the extent and age of the universe, and mathematical concepts underlying cosmological models.
Relevant to the dating of significant events in the history of the universe and the cosmological time scale.
Relevant to the origin and development of components of the universe and the conditions that led to planetary systems.
Covers the observational instruments used to study the cosmos and determine the large-scale structure of the universe.
Relevant to gravitational theory, the gravitational constant, and gravity measurements discussed in this section.
Relevant to the historical development of our understanding of the cosmos and early contributions to gravitational theory.
Relevant to the historical development of gravitational concepts and early telescopic observations of the cosmos.
